query: epidural analgesia for surgical management of peritoneal carcinomatosis

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural analgesia is indicated for pain management during or after surgical procedures involving the regions previously mentioned, severe chronic pain in the context of malignancy, and for labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Anesthesia -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A meta-analysis comparing intravenous and epidural routes of patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) in abdominal surgery demonstrated that epidural PCA was associated with better postoperative pain relief, reduced opioid consumption, and fewer postoperative complications than intravenous PCA. [10] Additionally, combined epidural anesthesia and analgesia reduced postoperative cognitive impairment in general anesthesia patients. [11] Furthermore, investigations have been conducted on the effects of perioperative epidural analgesia on cancer recurrence and survival, suggesting a potential association between epidural analgesia and improved survival outcomes. [12] However, recent studies have shown decreased benefits of epidural analgesia during surgery compared to catheter wound infusions and peripheral nerve blocks with a higher risk of complications. [4] In limited studies, epidural anesthesia demonstrated slightly more effective postsurgical pain control than systemic medications. Therefore, other than for obstetrical indications, epidural anesthesia may be more useful as an adjuvant therapy; further research is needed before definitive conclusions can be reached. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural anesthesia can be used to supplement general anesthesia or as the main anesthesia method in certain surgical procedures involving thoracic, abdominal, pelvic, or lower extremities regions. However, spinal anesthesia is more commonly used in these instances.

retrieved_docs.title: Erector Spinae Plane Block -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Comparative studies further underscore the clinical value of erector spinae plane block across diverse surgical settings. Although the Pecs II block was found to provide better analgesia and lower opioid requirements than erector spinae plane block in breast cancer surgery [14] , erector spinae plane remains a less invasive and technically simpler alternative in many thoracic procedures. When compared with paravertebral blocks in patients undergoing percutaneous nephrolithotomy, erector spinae plane blocks offered equivalent analgesia but were significantly faster and easier to perform, making them a practical option, especially in resource-constrained or high-throughput environments. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraxial anesthesia allows the possibility of performing surgical procedures without putting the patient under general anesthesia. As a result, neuraxial anesthesia decreases the use of intravenous opioids in the perioperative period and their effect on the patient, including respiratory depression, nausea, ileus, pruritus, etc. Also, it eliminated ventilator-associated complications and allowed to evaluate the patient's neurological status.

retrieved_docs.title: Postoperative Pain Control -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Neuraxial anesthetic techniques: This typically involves an epidural injection with local anesthetic with or without the addition of IV opioid medication; this may also include the intrathecal (spinal) injection of opioid medicines. Epidural analgesia may be given as a continuous infusion or as patient-controlled analgesia. These techniques are routinely used in major thoracic and abdominal procedures, cesarean sections, and hip or lower extremity surgeries. They are especially beneficial in patients at risk for cardiac or pulmonary complications or prolonged ileus. [2] Nonpharmacologic therapies: Nonpharmacologic therapies used in pain control include cognitive and mechanical modalities such as transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Erector Spinae Plane Block -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The erector spinae plane block has emerged as a clinically significant regional anesthetic technique due to its broad dermatomal coverage, favorable safety profile, and technical simplicity. It can provide effective analgesia for thoracic, abdominal, and some lower extremity procedures, making it a versatile option in perioperative pain management. [10] [11] While much of the early data on erector spinae plane originated from case reports and anecdotal experience, an expanding body of randomized controlled trials now supports its role in improving surgical outcomes. For example, in patients undergoing laparoscopic colon cancer surgery, bilateral erector spinae plane blocks were shown to significantly lower pain scores, reduce intraoperative neuromuscular blocker requirements, promote earlier ambulation and oral intake, and shorten hospital length of stay. [12] In cardiac surgery, erector spinae plane block was found to be superior to intravenous dexmedetomidine in off-pump coronary artery bypass grafting, reducing both pain scores and opioid consumption, prolonging time to first rescue analgesia, and shortening ICU stay. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Catheter (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural anesthesia can be used as an adjunct or primary anesthetic. Epidural anesthesia can be advantageous in cases where a prolonged duration of analgesia is anticipated or the need for titration is favorable. Epidural anesthesia can also be considered for patients with difficult airways or other concerns for respiratory compromise secondary to the effects of general anesthesia. Common uses of epidural anesthesia include obstetric, abdominal, urogenital, or lower extremity procedures. While upper abdominal procedures are feasible with epidural anesthesia, it is less common due to the difficulty in safely achieving anesthesia to the desired sensory level. [1] [4] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidural Morphine -- Indications -- Clinical Uses of Epidural Morphine
retrieved_docs.content: Epidural morphine finds application in various clinical scenarios, as listed below. Perioperative analgesia for several procedures, including: Abdominal surgeries Thoracic surgeries Vascular surgeries Gynecological surgeries Orthopedic procedures of the lower extremities, such as knee replacement surgery and hip arthroplasty Obstetrics settings, including: Cesarean section Labor pain Moderate-to-severe chronic pain, which is refractory to conservative treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Segmental Spinal Anesthesia -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Operations that have been performed with success include abdominal cancer surgeries, breast cancer surgeries, and laparoscopic cholecystectomies. In some of these surgeries, the procedure was performed in healthy individuals with excellent outcomes, so, in the future, the procedure may provide benefits for healthy patients as well. The safety of this technique needs to be confirmed by further studies involving a larger number of patients before it can be advised for routine use.

retrieved_docs.title: Flail Chest -- Treatment / Management -- Postoperative Care
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical wounds are closed in layers, and a chest tube is placed when the pleural cavity is entered. Perioperative analgesia may be provided using thoracic epidural catheters.

retrieved_docs.title: Perioperative Management of Pheochromocytoma -- Technique or Treatment -- Anesthesia Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be pre-medicated with anti-anxiety medication like midazolam. An arterial line is preferred before induction as induction may result in hemodynamic lability. General anesthesia alone or with an epidural is used for PCC resection. Epidural may be considered if open surgery is planned to provide adequate pain management in the postoperative period. Good IV access with two peripheral lines is necessary. Intra-operative transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE)/Pulmonary artery catheter may be considered in patients with severe cardiomyopathy to guide fluid management/inotrope administration or to assess ventricular function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2037
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a broad range of operative techniques in which surgeons directly examine an anatomic region to diagnose, treat, or remove pathology. The category includes adrenalectomy—partial or complete removal of the adrenal gland performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy—and may also involve excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. In hepatobiliary surgery, cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, is classified as an exploration method, as is choledochotomy or choledochostomy performed for ductal exploration, drainage, or stone removal, sometimes accompanied by transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by craniectomy or craniotomy performed for supratentorial investigation and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at exploring or decompressing cranial nerves. Spinal exploration procedures consist of laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these are specified for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), and thoracic regions, and for more than two thoracic segments, with the latter variations noted as possibly included methods. Urologic and endocrine exploration includes radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration. The broader classification “Clinical actions A‑G” serves as a parent category for exploration procedures, indicating that these diverse surgical methods are organized under a common clinical action framework. Together, these procedures illustrate how direct anatomical exploration is integral to the diagnosis, management, and definitive treatment of diseases across multiple organ systems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative methods. It includes arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, several types of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty with or without cholangiography), exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial, laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and disc excision at a single lumbar interspace, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration combined with mediastinal approaches such as sternal split, transthoracic access, or cervical mediastinal exploration. Excision also serves as the methodological basis for other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as for percutaneous nephrolithotomy (perkutánní nefrolitotomie). In addition, Excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to reconstructive nasal surgery. Certain extensive spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, namely laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy when more than two vertebral segments are involved in either the cervical or lumbar spine. The hierarchical structure places Clinical actions A‑G as a parent group under Excision, and Excision is also referenced in a colorectal‑cancer diagnostic protocol, highlighting its role in broader clinical pathways. Overall, Excision functions as a central concept that unites diverse operative techniques while delineating specific exclusions and connections to related procedural domains.

retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1051
retrieved_docs.content: Excision; lysis peritoneal adhesions is a surgical procedure that is performed indirectly on the peritoneum to release adhesions, and it is classified as one of the العمليات الجراحية في البطن (abdominal surgeries). The category العمليات الجراحية في البطن also includes العمليات الجراحية لقناة فالوب (Fallopian tube surgeries), indicating that surgeries of the Fallopian tube are a subset of abdominal operations. The peritoneum serves as the anatomical site where فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) is identified and is involved in اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ (peritoneal disorder). Clinical observations show that العمليات الجراحية في البطن often co‑occur with الْتِهابُ الصِّفاقِ (peritonitis), underscoring the close link between operative intervention in the abdomen and inflammation of the peritoneal lining. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in surgical planning, accurate diagnosis of hernias, management of peritoneal disorders, and prevention or early treatment of postoperative peritonitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1572
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is identified as the response to the colorectal‑cancer diagnostic program “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX,” indicating its role in the work‑up of colorectal malignancy. In the context of surgical classification, Excision explicitly excludes several laminectomy procedures that involve exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—specifically the cervical, lumbar (except for spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic 1‑ or 2‑segment laminectomies. Conversely, Excision encompasses a broad range of operative methods, including Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct (procedure), Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or removal of calculus (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), Craniectomy or craniotomy, exploratory; infratentorial (posterior fossa), Laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with decompression of nerve root(s), including partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of herniated intervertebral disc, re‑exploration, single interspace (cervical), Nipple exploration with excision of lactiferous duct, Orchiectomy, radical, for tumor with abdominal exploration, Parathyroidectomy or exploration of parathyroid(s), and Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration by transthoracic approach. Additionally, Excision is described as a “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it is linked to “plastic procedures on nose.” Together, these relationships portray Excision as a versatile procedural concept that is applied to specific therapeutic surgeries, excluded from certain spinal laminectomies, and connected to both diagnostic pathways for colorectal cancer and reconstructive or therapeutic interventions of the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures comprise a diverse group of surgical interventions that are performed to investigate, access, and treat pathology in multiple anatomic regions. In the endocrine system, both partial and complete adrenalectomy, as well as adrenal gland exploration with or without biopsy, can be performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, and may include removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy combined with common bile duct exploration, which may be performed with cholangiography, and choledochotomy or choledochostomy procedures that incorporate ductal exploration, drainage, calculus removal, and optional transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty. Neurosurgical exploration is represented by exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial region. Orthopedic exploration focuses on laminectomy with spinal cord or cauda equina exploration and decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy; these techniques are applied to one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, lumbar (excluding spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic regions, and may be extended to more than two segments in the cervical and lumbar spine. Minimally invasive exploration includes laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common duct exploration. Urologic exploration is exemplified by radical orchiectomy for tumor removal accompanied by abdominal exploration. Finally, parathyroid exploration procedures involve parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed either through a cervical approach or a transthoracic approach. Some of these procedures are noted as having possibly included methods, indicating that variations or additional steps may be incorporated depending on the clinical scenario. Together, these exploration procedures reflect a comprehensive surgical strategy to diagnose, access, and manage disease across multiple organ systems.
